title: High-order harmonic generation from an atom in a disordered environment
authors: Simon His, Camille Lévêque, Jérémie Caillat, Richard Taïeb, Jonathan Dubois
abstract: Using one-dimensional simulations analyzed through the lens of open quantum systems, we study the photoelectron's strong-field dynamics from an atom surrounded by a scattering environment stochastically structured. We theoretically investigate high-order harmonic generation from this situation. We show that local dephasing of the photoelectron wavepacket induced by elastic scattering leads to global decoherence. This drives a transition from quantum to classical behavior, as witnessed by the photoelectron probability density localizing around specific trajectories of the classical analog system: unstable periodic orbits. This phenomenon mirrors quantum scars traditionally observed in the eigenfunctions of time-independent systems, such as quantum billiards. Here, it emerges in-situ within a time-dependent framework, manifesting directly in the real-time dynamics from the ground state rather than solely through spectral analysis.
summary: This paper studies how a disordered, liquid-like environment modifies high-order harmonic generation by inducing decoherence. It demonstrates that scattering from the environment leads to a secondary spectral plateau and the emergence of 'dynamic scars' where the electron density localizes around classical periodic orbits.
main_problem: Investigating how a stochastically structured, disordered environment (simulating a liquid phase) affects high-order harmonic generation (HHG) and drives the transition from quantum to classical behavior.
main_result: The disordered environment induces global decoherence and a secondary HHG plateau due to off-site recombination, causing the electron probability density to localize around unstable classical periodic orbits (dynamic scars).
method: Numerical solution of the 1D time-dependent Schrödinger equation using a symplectic integrator, combined with an ensemble average over stochastic configurations to represent the density matrix.
model_or_system: A single atom with a soft-Coulomb potential in a 1D environment containing a stochastic distribution of Gaussian-well perturbers, driven by a strong laser field.
key_observables: HHG intensity spectra, harmonic amplitudes/cutoff, system purity, and photoelectron probability density.
important_parameters: Laser intensity (7.896e14 W/cm2), wavelength (1030 nm), mean inter-scatterer distance (10 a.u.), and ionization potential (0.90 a.u.).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled in one dimension to isolate elastic scattering, and the environment is treated via ensemble averaging rather than a Markovian reservoir.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the pair-correlation function of the scatterers; Figure 6 illustrates the decay of purity over time; Figure 9 shows the spatial component of the flow for classical trajectories.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of HHG in liquids, describes the 1D stochastic model and numerical methods, analyzes the resulting HHG spectra and decoherence dynamics, and concludes by linking the results to the concept of quantum scars.
why_interesting: It provides a mechanistic link between structural disorder and decoherence in a time-dependent framework, extending the concept of quantum scars from static systems to driven, open-system dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Pair-correlation function, following the joint proba- bility distribution (10), as a function of the distance between two scatterers.

Figure 2. HHG intensity spectra obtained from the modulus of the Fourier transform of (13) as a function of the harmonic number for a laser intensity 7.896 × 1014 W cm−2, a laser wavelength of 1030 nm, and a 2-11-2 trapezoidal envelope. (a) Gas phase with AE = 0 in Eq. (4). (b) Single configuration of the disordered environment (Nc = 1) and (c) liquid phase with a thousand configurations (Nc = 103), with AE = 0.8 a.u. and σE = 0.5 a.u. The vertical dashed lines indicate harmonics 21 and 227. The insets are zooms on the harmonics [150, 170] and intensities [10−15, 10−5]. The vertical dotted lines indicate the odd harmonics.
Figure 3 shows a time-frequency map of the dipole ac- celeration d(t) in Eq. (13) for the gas phase (panel a) and the liquid phase (panel b). It corresponds to the modulus of the Gabor transform
Figure 3. Time frequency analyses of the harmonic of the dipole [see Eq. (14)] for (a) the gas phase and (b) the liquid phase. The parameters are the same as in Fig. 2.

Figure 4. Same as Fig. 3 but with the kinetic energies of the electron as a function of the recombination time in the SFA. The solid lines are for ionization during the first laser cycle after the plateau. The dotted line are for later times, i.e. they correspond to the solid line translated by integer multiples of the half-period. The different colors correspond to different distances of return site with |x(tr)| = ℓ: black is for the return at ℓ= 0, dark gray is at ℓ= a the mean inter-scatterer distance, lighter gray is ℓ= 2a, etc.
